text: The surgeon traces the jejunum proximally to the ligament of Treitz using DaVinci Firefly fluorescence imaging to illuminate the colonic vasculature. A vessel sealer enters the transverse mesocolon under fluoroscopic guidance. The third portion of the duodenum is identified and partially Kocherized. A tension-free segment of the proximal jejunum is positioned over the third portion of the duodenum for anastomosis, with a 3-0 V-Loc suture used to create the back wall of the anastomosis.

The surgeon uses an XI permanent pottery hook to create incisions for the jejunotomy and duodenotomy on the anti-mesenteric wall of the small bowel. A SureForm 60mm XI reloadable stapler is then used to secure the duodenal jejunal anastomosis, with the thick arm placed in the jejunotomy and the thin arm in the duodenotomy. After ensuring no redundancy in the small bowel tissue, the stapler is clamped and fired. The duodenal jejunostomy is closed, and the stapled anastomosis is over-sewn with 3-0 V-lock sutures. The mesenteric defect is closed with a 2-0 V-lock suture to prevent future herniation.

The surgeon sprays five milliliters of Tis-Seal fibrin sealant onto the anastomosis to secure it. The anastomosis is then irrigated with normal saline, and an endoscopic leak test is performed, showing no bubbles, indicating a negative leak test.
